phase1.explanation:
The Executor Framework manages thread creation and pooling:

ExecutorService:
• Manages a pool of threads
• Reuses threads efficiently
• Handles task queuing

Creating executors:
• Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor() — single thread
• Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n) — fixed number of threads
• Executors.newCachedThreadPool() — creates threads as needed, reuses idle ones
• Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(n) — schedule tasks

Submitting tasks:
• execute(Runnable) — fire and forget
• submit(Callable) — returns Future
• invokeAll(Collection<Callable>) — execute all, get all results

Future:
• get() — blocks until result ready
• get(timeout, unit) — blocks with timeout
• isDone() — check if complete
• cancel() — cancel task

Scheduled tasks:
• schedule(Runnable, delay, unit) — run once after delay
• scheduleAtFixedRate(...) — run periodically

Shutting down:
• shutdown() — no new tasks, finish existing
• shutdownNow() — attempt to stop all
• awaitTermination() — wait for termination

Benefits:
• Better than manual thread creation
• Reuses threads (performance)
• Simplified thread management

⚠️ Always shutdown executors to prevent resource leaks!

phase2.errorMessage:
Done

(No error — but the program won't exit! The thread pool keeps the JVM alive.)

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates a FixedThreadPool with 4 threads
2. Submits 10 tasks that print "Task X on thread Y" with X being 1-10
3. Uses Future to submit tasks and get results
4. Shuts down the pool properly

Bonus: Use submit() with Callable that returns the task number.
dialogueHints: shutdown() after all tasks are submitted
phase3.openingDialogue: Executor Framework — manage threads like a pro! 🎯
